does: This paper systematically evaluates large language models, specifically GPT-3.5-mini, as academic peer reviewers by comparing their review outputs against human reviewers on a dataset of 1,441 papers from ICLR 2023 and NeurIPS 2022. The authors employ structured prompting with reference paper calibration, topic modeling, and similarity analysis to assess whether LLMs produce ratings, strengths, and weaknesses comparable to human judgments. They additionally conduct adversarial evaluation by embedding covert prompt injection instructions into PDF submissions to test LLM robustness. The key findings reveal that LLMs systematically inflate ratings for weaker papers while providing better alignment with human reviewers on stronger contributions, and that while overarching malicious prompts cause only minor topical shifts, certain field-specific injection techniques achieve more substantial effects.
connects: This work directly responds to the emerging practice of deploying LLMs as reviewer aids in academic publishing, building on earlier research into AI-assisted decision-making systems and their potential biases. It extends prior work on LLM reliability and consistency by applying systematic evaluation frameworks developed for assessing automated systems in high-stakes domains. The paper connects to literature on prompt injection attacks and adversarial robustness in language models, as well as research examining fairness and calibration issues in AI-assisted evaluation. It also draws on established peer review methodology and bibliometric analysis techniques to operationalize comparison between human and machine reviewers. The work addresses concerns raised in earlier studies about the limitations of LLMs in nuanced, expert judgment tasks.
enables: This evaluation establishes a foundation for understanding when and how LLMs might safely augment rather than replace human reviewers, pointing toward hybrid review systems that leverage machine assistance while maintaining human oversight. The identified vulnerabilities to prompt injection attacks motivate future work on securing LLM-based review systems against adversarial manipulation, potentially through robust prompt design or additional verification layers. The systematic bias patterns uncovered—particularly rating inflation for weaker papers—suggest avenues for calibration techniques that could improve LLM alignment with human judgment. The paper enables policy discussions around responsible deployment of AI in peer review and sets benchmarks for evaluating future reviewer models. It also opens questions about designing review systems that account for both capabilities and failure modes of current language models.
